description: If a relation is transitive, it chains — R(a,b) and R(b,c) give R(a,c).
goal: (-> (forall x (forall y (forall z (-> (and (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) z)) (App (App R x) z))))) (-> (and (App (App R a) b) (App (App R b) c)) (App (App R a) c)))

hints.text: **Transitivity** is a classic relation property: if $x$ relates to $y$, and $y$ relates to $z$, then $x$ relates to $z$. Think 'is an ancestor of' or 'is less than'. The goal mirrors problem 49 (symmetry) but with three nested $\forall$ quantifiers and a [conjunction|conjunction] in the antecedent. Your proof structure: assume transitivity, assume $R(a,b) \land R(b,c)$, then instantiate the transitivity property three times to reach $R(a,c)$.

hints.tactic: (assume (forall x (forall y (forall z (-> (and (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) z)) (App (App R x) z))))))
(assume (and (App (App R a) b) (App (App R b) c)))

hints.text: The result at step 5 is $R(a,b) \land R(b,c) \to R(a,c)$. Step 2 gives the [antecedent|antecedent]. Apply [modus-ponens|modus ponens].

hints.tactic: (->-intro (and (App (App R a) b) (App (App R b) c)) 6)
(->-intro (forall x (forall y (forall z (-> (and (App (App R x) y) (App (App R y) z)) (App (App R x) z))))) 7)
